records.ety: [L. vulgaris, from vulgus the multitude, the common people; of uncertain origin: cf. F. vulgaire. Cf. Divulge.]

defs: Of or pertaining to the mass, or multitude, of people; common; general; ordinary; public; hence, in general use; vernacular.
quotes: Things vulgar, and well-weighed, scarce worth the praise.
It might be more useful to the English reader . . . to write in our vulgar language.
The mechanical process of multiplying books had brought the New Testament in the vulgar tongue within the reach of every class.

defs: Belonging or relating to the common people, as distinguished from the cultivated or educated; pertaining to common life; plebeian; not select or distinguished; hence, sometimes, of little or no value.
quotes: Men who have passed all their time in low and vulgar life.
In reading an account of a battle, we follow the hero with our whole attention, but seldom reflect on the vulgar heaps of slaughter.

defs: Hence, lacking cultivation or refinement; rustic; boorish; also, offensive to good taste or refined feelings; low; coarse; mean; base; as, vulgar men, minds, language, or manners.
quotes: Be thou familiar, but by no means vulgar.

defs: One of the common people; a vulgar person.
quotes: These vile vulgars are extremely proud.
